storm.summary_layperson: Bands of rain and a few rumbles of thunder are circling the cove from the north tonight, with locally heavy rainfall — not severe storms — as the main concern.
storm.local_effect_blurb: Sound water (69°F) is nearly the same temperature as the air, so there is no cooling marine layer, and storms approaching from the NNW travel over land rather than across the Sound — the cove gets no special protection tonight.
storm.detail_html:
The 4:24 PM radar shows an active cluster of showers and embedded cells ringing the cove from the north and northwest. The nearest echo boundary is roughly 7.5 mi NNW with peak reflectivity near 46 dBZ, while a loose arc of cells stretches from NNW through NNE at 10–20 mi out. The system as a whole is drifting slowly ENE at 7.3 kt — barely closing on the cove. Most of the higher-reflectivity cells (41–42 dBZ) to the north are moving away; only one cell 11 mi NW has a positive closing speed (2.5 kt).

Tonight's storms approach from the NNW over land, so Long Island Sound offers no protective buffer. The Sound's deflection mechanism — which can weaken or split incoming cells — only engages when storms cross the cool water from the southwest. An NNW land approach bypasses it entirely, meaning the cove faces similar conditions to surrounding inland towns. No marine layer is present: Sound water (69°F) is slightly warmer than the air (66°F), the opposite of the cool-water protective scenario.

Despite the active radar picture, instability is essentially zero — CAPE is 10 J/kg, and the NWS Area Forecast Discussion notes that "instability is very limited over the local area, hindering the development of torrential rainfall rates." An active Flood Watch is in effect through Tuesday 6 AM, driven by high atmospheric moisture (~2-inch PWATs) and slow storm motion rather than explosive convective development. The NWS forecast calls for a quarter to half inch of rain with locally higher amounts possible.

The closing cell (11 mi NW, 36 dBZ) is predicted to reach the cove around 8:16 PM, with a wide window of 7:18–9:14 PM. The wide range reflects the very slow closing speed — even small wobbles in storm track shift arrival by 30–45 minutes. No marine-layer or sea-breeze adjustments were applied to the window. Motion is radar-measured (centroid tracking), so confidence is medium.

What would change the call: a sudden upstream gust spike at N1TKS (Port Chester, WSW) or GW4034 would signal an outflow boundary triggering new convection. Conversely, the low-CAPE environment favors cells continuing to weaken as they drift east — the highest reflectivities may stay north and only light rain reach the cove.

arrival.blurb: Radar-tracked cell 11 mi NW closing slowly at 2.5 kt — a diffuse, slow-moving rain system rather than an organized line, so exact timing is uncertain within the window.

hourly.shift_note: steady, building with storm circulation nearby
peak, veering ENE near storm complex

wind.tactics_blurb: Gradient-only NE–ENE flow at 12–16 kt with active storm cells to the north — not a racing evening. If conditions clear, NNE–NE offshore air is fairly uniform across the cove; stay center or shade west, as Greenwich Point shelters the east shore from ENE flow.
wind.tactics_detail_html:
Regime: B-moderate (gradient-only). The thermal driver is absent — Sound water at 69°F is slightly warmer than inland air at 66°F, a negative temperature differential (−3°F) that eliminates sea-breeze potential. Sea-breeze fractions are 0% across all subregions. Wind is pure synoptic gradient flow, reinforced by the mesoscale circulation around the storm complex to the north. Regime B wind does not collapse at sunset — it holds through the evening and beyond.

Direction uncertainty is high. HRRR forecasts NE (~40°) for the 17:00–22:00 window, but current observations show a significant eastward bias: the western LIS ensemble median sits at ESE 107°, KHVN reports ENE 60°, KHPN reports E 90°. This 73° model–obs divergence likely reflects storm-driven mesoscale circulation rather than the background synoptic flow. As the storm complex drifts away ENE, direction should gradually veer toward HRRR's NE forecast — expect ENE to NE (50–80°) through most of the window, trending NNE after 21:00. The HRRR directions are used for the forecast table; actual direction may remain more easterly than shown through 19:00.

Speed and gust character: HRRR peaks at ~16 kt around 18:00 EDT then diminishes to ~11 kt by 21:00. The current authoritative mean (10.5 kt across METARs) is below the peak, consistent with a building gradient. Gust estimates (~1.4× mean) are consistent with the current KHVN gust ratio (17/11 kt). Direction agreement across the ensemble is 0.78 — reasonably coherent for an active storm environment — but the high APRS coefficient of variation (1.09) confirms the APRS stations are fragmented, reflecting local shelter and storm effects. Expect persistent flow rather than oscillating shifts; there is no thermal driver to produce regular sea-breeze oscillations.

Cove geometry with NE–ENE flow: offshore air is clean and laminar in the NE quadrant. The east shore (near Greenwich Point / Tod's Point) receives partial shelter when flow is ENE or E — the start line will be lighter there than mid-cove. Center to slight west is the favored position. As flow backs toward NNE after 21:00, it becomes more uniform across the cove.

Fleet impact: 12–16 kt is powered-up dinghy territory, with gusts to 23 kt at peak — survival-mode for lighter classes. However, with storm cells within 10 mi, a Flood Watch active, and rain already falling at surrounding stations, sailing tonight is inadvisable. If conditions clear after 21:00, the diminishing NNE 10–11 kt gradient flow would be raceable for most classes.

data_caveats: RYC WeatherLink observation is 84 min old — cove-specific wind and temperature unverifiable
Buoy 44040 reading is 76 min old — over-water speed and direction may not reflect current conditions

HRRR forecast direction (NE ~40°) diverges 73° from current observations (ENE ~107°) — direction uncertainty high; forecast table uses HRRR but actual direction may remain more easterly through 19:00 EDT
